Task id: T106 (ledgerRefs goals:G6) — ROUND 2 re-dispatch after review.
Branch implement/T106 (HEAD a625953), worktree /home/pavel/work/safe/fecalia/.claude/worktrees/agent-a45dbb6e85fe5dcff. Work THERE if reachable; else `git switch -c t106-fix implement/T106` in your own worktree via the shared .git store, commit, report the EXACT sha (copy from git output).
Go repo — gate `go build ./... && go vet ./... && go test ./...` via `nix develop -c sh -c '...'`.

ONE prior-round criticism (autonomously-fixable):
The AddPath/deferred-reconcile device-mode wiring is UNVERIFIED: mutating resolveForcedDeviceBind to unconditionally return "" (making the entire runtime-path device-bind activation inert) passes the FULL bind suite. The task text says the mode "applies to Open, AddPath, and deferred-path reconcile", but only the Open path (selectDeviceBinds) currently has discriminating tests.
FIX: split resolveForcedDeviceBind's DECISION from the net.Interfaces() snapshot (mirror the planPathBinds/selectDeviceBinds injection seam already used for the Open path — pass the interface-info snapshot in rather than calling net.Interfaces() inline, so the decision is unit-testable without real interfaces). Unit-test all three modes THROUGH that seam: source -> "", auto -> "", device+resolvable -> the dev, device+unresolvable -> "" (fallback). AND assert the dev argument is threaded into deferredListen for a BindModeDevice deferred path via the existing fakeDeferredBinder (reconcile_test.go). Mutation-verify: neutering resolveForcedDeviceBind to return "" MUST now fail your new test.

SCOPE: only close the coverage gap for the AddPath/reconcile device-mode wiring (+ refactor resolveForcedDeviceBind to an injectable seam if needed). Do NOT add the device-fallback WARN log — that is filed separately as D53 (needs a logger threaded through NewMultipath, out of scope). Keep auto byte-for-byte and the existing tests green. Re-run the gate. Commit, return the structured result json {taskId, status, resultCommit, branch, filesTouched, checkSummary, summary, blockedReason?} with the exact resultCommit sha.

- baboon: Baboon data model compiler reference. Covers model syntax, multi-version evolution, code generation, wire-format conversions, database migrations, backward compatibility, and version negotiation. TRIGGER when: designing, changing, or discussing any external API, working with .baboon model files, updating data models, adding new fields or types, bumping model versions, running code generation, implementing conversions, writing database migrations, or discussing API versioning strategy.
- constructive-test-taxonomy: Multi-axis classification of tests (Intention × Encapsulation × Isolation) for evaluating test quality and steering test-writing decisions. Replaces fuzzy Unit/Integration/E2E vocabulary with precise tags and a maintenance-cost heuristic. TRIGGER when: writing new tests, reviewing a test suite, deciding what to test or at what level, discussing mocks vs. real dependencies, classifying tests, or arguing about test coverage or test-strategy tradeoffs.
- dual-tests: Pattern for testing code that touches external systems: write one abstract test suite against an interface, then run it against both the production adapter (real DB/API/FS) and a hand-written in-memory dummy. Prefers manual dummies over auto-generated mocks. TRIGGER when: designing code that depends on a database, HTTP API, message queue, filesystem, or other external system; choosing between mocks, fakes, or dummies; adding integration points; setting up test infrastructure; or when the `constructive-test-taxonomy` skill calls for moving Communication-tier tests toward Blackbox-Atomic.
- environment: Bubblewrap "yolo" sandbox escape workflow (exchange scripts for reaching $HOME, system paths, or files outside the sandbox) plus in-sandbox nix/direnv usage. TRIGGER only when $SMIND_SANDBOXED=1 — i.e. running inside the yolo sandbox and you need to reach something outside it. SKIP entirely when $SMIND_SANDBOXED is unset: you are unsandboxed, so write directly and ignore this skill.
- izumi: Best-practice playbook for the izumi Scala ecosystem — distage (DI), distage-framework (roles/RoleAppMain), distage-testkit (test harness with memoization), distage-framework-docker (test containers), logstage (structured logging), and BIO (bifunctor effect typeclasses). Covers idiomatic bindings, Activation/Axis switching, Lifecycle discipline, plugin layout, and how to apply the `dual-tests` pattern inside distage so one test body runs against both a dummy and a real-DB adapter. TRIGGER when: writing or reviewing Scala code that imports `izumi.distage.*`, `izumi.logstage.*`, `izumi.functional.bio.*`, `distage.*`, `logstage.*`; designing a new distage plugin or Role; wiring a testkit `SpecZIO`/`Spec2`/`Spec1`; configuring `ContainerDef`/`DockerSupportModule`; choosing between `StandardAxis.Repo` or `Scene` tags; or implementing the dual-tests pattern in a distage project. The canonical reference project is https://github.com/7mind/distage-example.
- resilient-ws-ui: Guidelines for building applications that maintain reliable WebSocket connections to a backend and surface connection health clearly to the user. Invoke ONLY when the user explicitly references this skill by name (e.g. "/resilient-ws-ui", "use the resilient-ws-ui skill"). Do NOT auto-invoke based on topic match.
- tass: Target/Actual State Separation (TASS) pattern for reliable state management in event-driven control systems. TRIGGER when: implementing device or entity state management, designing control systems, working with target vs actual state separation, or building event-driven systems with commanded/observed state.
- cq:advance: Advance the WHOLE flow one cycle — chain /cq:investigate:advance, /cq:plan:advance, /cq:implement:advance to quiescence, then report DRAINED / BLOCKED-ON-QUESTIONS / MIXED.
- cq:implement-review: Implement-flow adversarial per-task review rubric + stdout-JSON contract, as a portable promptTemplate for non-Claude harnesses (pi/Codex). Symmetric to cq:plan-review. Judges ONE task's implementation, splits findings into autonomously-fixable criticism[], user-only questions[], and out-of-scope defects[], and emits the verdict as JSON on stdout. Writes NOTHING to the ledger — the orchestrator records the terminal review.
- cq:implement:advance: Advance an implement-flow run one full pass — pick DAG-ready tasks, implement each in an isolated worktree, adversarially review, autonomously fix criticism, register user questions, and merge back in dependency order.
- cq:implement:start: Start an implement-flow run — resolve the target milestones (default: all open ones), validate their task DAG, then hand off to the /cq:implement:advance loop.
- cq:investigate: Start an investigate-flow run — defect intake + bootstrap, then run the /cq:investigate:advance pass inline.
- cq:investigate:advance: Advance an investigate-flow run one research round — read defect state, form/extend the hypothesis tree, dispatch read-only explorers, validate every citation against source, adjudicate node status, and on a confirmed root cause file-and-defer the fix to plan-flow.
- cq:plan: Start a plan-flow goal — create the goal (from free text OR one-or-more idea-ids, one goal per idea), then hand off to the planner for the first clarifying questions.
- cq:plan-review: Shared adversarial plan-review rubric — judge an emitted plan for a goal against the fine-grained / sequenced / testable / grounded / complete rubric, classify findings into new_questions / criticism / defects, and emit a single fenced-json verdict on stdout. The CANONICAL rubric source; Claude's plan-reviewer agent and every non-Claude (Codex/Pi) reviewer judge the same way and emit the same JSON.
- cq:plan:advance: Advance plan-flow goals one full round — a given goal, or (no argument) every unlocked goal — running the planner↔reviewer loop until each needs the user or reaches `planned`.
- cq:plan:follow-up: Add new scope to an EXISTING plan-flow goal — append the follow-up request, re-open the goal, and hand to the planner for a fresh clarifying round.
- cq:planners: Session-only planner-set override — parse a natural-language planner instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_planners/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- cq:reviewers: Session-only reviewer-set override — parse a natural-language reviewer instruction (e.g. "use grok and opus only") into canonical harness:model tokens via the cq.toml [aliases] table (from the ledger MCP get_reviewers/get_config) plus a documented fallback map; echo the resolved active set; confirm the override is SESSION-ONLY and reverts on the next fresh run.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- codex:rescue: Delegate investigation, an explicit fix request, or follow-up rescue work to the Codex rescue subagent
- codex:setup: Check whether the local Codex CLI is ready and optionally toggle the stop-time review gate
- codex:codex-cli-runtime: Internal helper contract for calling the codex-companion runtime from Claude Code
- codex:codex-result-handling: Internal guidance for presenting Codex helper output back to the user
- codex:gpt-5-4-prompting: Internal guidance for composing Codex and GPT-5.4 prompts for coding, review, diagnosis, and research tasks inside the Codex Claude Code plugin
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"context"
5		"fmt"
6		"net"
7		"net/netip"
8		"strconv"
9		"syscall"
10	
11		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
12	)
13	
14	// listenPath binds one path's UDP socket to port on the path's source.
15	//
16	// When dev is non-empty the socket is bound to that INTERFACE (SO_BINDTODEVICE)
17	// with a wildcard local address instead of pinning the specific source IP. That
18	// is what makes an edge's path survive a public-IP change mid-session (a re-roam
19	// / NAT rebind — T16): when the interface's address changes, a device-bound
20	// socket keeps sending, now sourced from the interface's NEW address, and the far
21	// side's Bind re-learns that source from the next authenticated probe (T37). A
22	// socket pinned to the OLD source IP would instead fail every send with
23	// ENETUNREACH once that address is removed, and the path could never recover
24	// without a re-Open (which would reset the WireGuard session).
25	//
26	// dev is chosen by planPathBinds / selectDeviceBinds and is non-empty ONLY when
27	// device binding is provably equivalent to pinning the configured source_addr and
28	// no other path contends for the device (see selectDeviceBinds). When dev is ""
29	// the socket pins the specific source IP — the pre-T16 behaviour.
30	//
31	// Device binding is also BEST-EFFORT even when selected: SO_BINDTODEVICE needs
32	// CAP_NET_RAW (the daemon runs privileged, but the unit tests bind loopback
33	// unprivileged) and is Linux-only, so a device-bind failure falls back to
34	// source-IP binding rather than failing Open.
35	func listenPath(src netip.Addr, port uint16, dev string) (*net.UDPConn, error) {
36		if dev != "" {
37			if c, err := listenOnDevice(src, port, dev); err == nil {
38				return c, nil
39			}
40			// SO_BINDTODEVICE denied / unsupported: fall back to source-IP binding.
41		}
42		laddr := &net.UDPAddr{IP: net.IP(src.AsSlice()), Port: int(port)}
43		return net.ListenUDP("udp", laddr)
44	}
45	
46	// ifaceInfo is the resolution of a source address against the host's interfaces:
47	// the non-loopback interface currently holding the address (dev, "" when none
48	// does) and how many addresses of that source's family (IPv4 vs IPv6) the
49	// interface carries. familyCount drives the device-bind decision — a device-bind
50	// socket is only equivalent to the configured source pin when the interface holds
51	// exactly one address of the family.
52	type ifaceInfo struct {
53		dev         string
54		familyCount int
55	}
56	
57	// planPathBinds resolves every path source against a SINGLE snapshot of the
58	// host's interfaces and returns the per-path bind plan (see selectDeviceBinds):
59	// index i holds the device to SO_BINDTODEVICE-bind path i to, or "" to pin path
60	// i's specific source IP. A snapshot failure resolves every source to an empty
61	// dev, so all paths (other than a forced BindModeDevice — see selectDeviceBinds)
62	// fall back to source-IP binding. modes is parallel to srcs and holds each
63	// path's RESOLVED bind mode (config.Path.Bind after normalize(), I5) — never
64	// empty on a loaded config, but selectDeviceBinds treats any value other than
65	// BindModeSource/BindModeDevice as BindModeAuto defensively.
66	func planPathBinds(srcs []netip.Addr, modes []config.BindMode) []string {
67		ifaces, err := net.Interfaces()
68		if err != nil {
69			ifaces = nil
70		}
71		return selectDeviceBinds(srcs, modes, func(s netip.Addr) ifaceInfo {
72			return interfaceInfo(s, ifaces)
73		})
74	}
75	
76	// selectDeviceBinds decides, per source address, whether its path socket may be
77	// bound to the source's interface (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard) or must pin the
78	// specific source IP, honoring each path's RESOLVED BindMode (I5, Q42):
79	//
80	//   - config.BindModeSource forces source-IP pinning unconditionally: the
81	//     source's interface is never even consulted for a device-bind decision.
82	//     This is the D38 escape hatch — a source-policy-routed uplink (one address
83	//     per VLAN interface) that the BindModeAuto heuristic below would otherwise
84	//     device-bind, silently defeating `ip rule from <source_addr>`.
85	//
86	//   - config.BindModeDevice forces a device bind unconditionally: the path's
87	//     interface is used whenever it resolves, regardless of family count or
88	//     contention with another configured path. An unresolvable interface (no
89	//     owning interface found) falls back to source-IP binding; a resolved-but-
90	//     failing SO_BINDTODEVICE (permission, unsupported) falls back the same way
91	//     at listenPath's setsockopt layer.
92	//
93	//   - config.BindModeAuto (and, defensively, any other/unset value) reproduces
94	//     the pre-I5 heuristic BYTE-FOR-BYTE: device binding — the T16 roam-
95	//     surviving mode — is chosen ONLY when it is provably equivalent to pinning
96	//     the configured source_addr AND no other path contends for the device:
97	//
98	//   - the source resolves to a non-loopback interface (dev != ""),
99	//
100	//   - that interface carries exactly ONE address of the source's family, so a
101	//     wildcard-on-device socket can only ever source from the configured address
102	//     (a multi-address interface would let the kernel pick a DIFFERENT source via
103	//     route-based selection, voiding source_addr's pin — Criticism 2), and
104	//
105	//   - exactly ONE configured path resolves to that interface, because two
106	//     wildcard+device sockets on the same port and device collide EADDRINUSE;
107	//     source-IP binding keeps their two DISTINCT specific-IP sockets, which
108	//     coexist on one port — the pre-T16 behaviour (Criticism 1).
109	//
110	// Every auto-mode path that fails those checks falls back to source-IP binding,
111	// exactly as before T16 (at the cost of not surviving a same-address readdress
112	// for those paths — the pre-T16 status quo). The returned slice is parallel to
113	// srcs: a non-empty dev at i means "device-bind path i to it"; "" means
114	// "source-IP-bind path i". modes is parallel to srcs.
115	func selectDeviceBinds(srcs []netip.Addr, modes []config.BindMode, resolve func(netip.Addr) ifaceInfo) []string {
116		infos := make([]ifaceInfo, len(srcs))
117		devPaths := make(map[string]int, len(srcs))
118		for i, s := range srcs {
119			infos[i] = resolve(s)
120			if infos[i].dev != "" {
121				devPaths[infos[i].dev]++
122			}
123		}
124		out := make([]string, len(srcs))
125		for i := range srcs {
126			info := infos[i]
127			switch modes[i] {
128			case config.BindModeSource:
129				// out[i] stays "": never SO_BINDTODEVICE, regardless of what the
130				// interface resolution would have decided (the D38 escape hatch).
131			case config.BindModeDevice:
132				// Forced device-bind: use the path's interface unconditionally when it
133				// resolves. info.dev is already "" when unresolvable, which is the
134				// fallback-to-source-IP-binding outcome.
135				out[i] = info.dev
136			default: // config.BindModeAuto, or any other/unset value.
137				if info.dev != "" && info.familyCount == 1 && devPaths[info.dev] == 1 {
138					out[i] = info.dev
139				}
140			}
141		}
142		return out
143	}
144	
145	// resolveForcedDeviceBind resolves src's interface for a single, isolated
146	// forced-device-mode bind (AddPath / the T55 deferred-path reconcile), which —
147	// unlike Open's planPathBinds/selectDeviceBinds — binds one path at a time with
148	// no other-path contention to check. It returns "" (source-IP-bind) for any mode
149	// other than config.BindModeDevice, or when the interface cannot be resolved, so
150	// the caller's listenPath call falls back to source-IP binding exactly as the
151	// unresolvable-interface case does in selectDeviceBinds.
152	func resolveForcedDeviceBind(src netip.Addr, mode config.BindMode) string {
153		if mode != config.BindModeDevice {
154			return ""
155		}
156		ifaces, err := net.Interfaces()
157		if err != nil {
158			return ""
159		}
160		return interfaceInfo(src, ifaces).dev
161	}
162	
163	// interfaceInfo resolves src against ifaces (a single net.Interfaces snapshot):
164	// it returns the non-loopback interface that currently owns src and the count of
165	// same-family addresses on it. A loopback/unspecified/invalid src, or no owning
166	// interface, yields an empty dev (familyCount 0). It is the resolution step that
167	// lets a source-address config select a device to bind, so the socket can outlive
168	// that address changing.
169	func interfaceInfo(src netip.Addr, ifaces []net.Interface) ifaceInfo {
170		if !src.IsValid() || src.IsLoopback() || src.IsUnspecified() {
171			return ifaceInfo{}
172		}
173		want := src.Unmap()
174		for _, ifc := range ifaces {
175			if ifc.Flags&net.FlagLoopback != 0 {
176				continue
177			}
178			addrs, err := ifc.Addrs()
179			if err != nil {
180				continue
181			}
182			ips := make([]netip.Addr, 0, len(addrs))
183			for _, a := range addrs {
184				ipn, ok := a.(*net.IPNet)
185				if !ok {
186					continue
187				}
188				ip, ok := netip.AddrFromSlice(ipn.IP)
189				if !ok {
190					continue
191				}
192				ips = append(ips, ip)
193			}
194			familyCount, owns := familyBindCount(want, ips)
195			if owns {
196				return ifaceInfo{dev: ifc.Name, familyCount: familyCount}
197			}
198		}
199		return ifaceInfo{}
200	}

1	package bind
2	
3	import (
4		"net/netip"
5		"testing"
6	
7		"github.com/7mind/wanbond/internal/config"
8	)
9	
10	// autoModes returns a config.BindMode slice of n BindModeAuto entries, the mode
11	// every pre-I5 selectDeviceBinds test exercised implicitly.
12	func autoModes(n int) []config.BindMode {
13		modes := make([]config.BindMode, n)
14		for i := range modes {
15			modes[i] = config.BindModeAuto
16		}
17		return modes
18	}
19	
20	// TestSelectDeviceBinds is the T16 rework regression for the device-bind SELECTION
21	// rule (the two review criticisms fable reproduced under `unshare -Urn`), now run
22	// under explicit BindModeAuto (I5) to pin it as the REGRESSION GUARD that auto
23	// reproduces the pre-I5 heuristic byte-for-byte on these fixtures. Device binding
24	// (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard source — the roam-surviving mode) must be chosen
25	// ONLY when it is provably equivalent to pinning the configured source_addr AND no
26	// other path contends for the device; every other path must fall back to
27	// source-IP binding (the pre-T16 behaviour that lets distinct specific-IP sockets
28	// coexist on one port). It exercises selectDeviceBinds with a fake resolver so it
29	// needs no privilege / real interfaces.
30	func TestSelectDeviceBinds(t *testing.T) {
31		addr := func(s string) netip.Addr { return netip.MustParseAddr(s) }
32	
33		// build wraps a source-address -> ifaceInfo table as a resolver.
34		build := func(table map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo) func(netip.Addr) ifaceInfo {
35			return func(a netip.Addr) ifaceInfo { return table[a] }
36		}
37	
38		tests := []struct {
39			name    string
40			srcs    []netip.Addr
41			table   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo
42			wantDev []string
43		}{
44			{
45				// A solo path on a single-address interface: device-bind (roam benefit).
46				name:    "solo single-address interface device-binds",
47				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("198.51.100.10")},
48				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{addr("198.51.100.10"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 1}},
49				wantDev: []string{"eth0"},
50			},
51			{
52				// Criticism 1: two paths whose sources live on the SAME interface. Neither
53				// may device-bind (the second wildcard+device socket collides EADDRINUSE);
54				// both source-IP-bind so their two distinct specific-IP sockets coexist.
55				name: "shared interface -> all source-IP-bound",
56				srcs: []netip.Addr{addr("192.0.2.10"), addr("192.0.2.11")},
57				table: map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{
58					addr("192.0.2.10"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2},
59					addr("192.0.2.11"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2},
60				},
61				wantDev: []string{"", ""},
62			},
63			{
64				// Criticism 2: a solo path on a MULTI-address interface. A wildcard+device
65				// socket would let the kernel pick a different source, voiding the pin, so
66				// it must source-IP-bind despite being the only path on the device.
67				name:    "solo multi-address interface -> source-IP-bound",
68				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("203.0.113.5")},
69				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{addr("203.0.113.5"): {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2}},
70				wantDev: []string{""},
71			},
72			{
73				// A source that resolves to no interface (loopback / unresolved) never
74				// device-binds — it falls straight through to source-IP binding.
75				name:    "unresolved source -> source-IP-bound",
76				srcs:    []netip.Addr{addr("127.0.0.1")},
77				table:   map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{},
78				wantDev: []string{""},
79			},
80			{
81				// The mixed real case: two solo single-address uplinks on distinct devices
82				// both device-bind, while a third pair sharing a device does not — the
83				// decision is per-path, not all-or-nothing.
84				name: "two solo uplinks device-bind, shared pair does not",
85				srcs: []netip.Addr{addr("198.51.100.10"), addr("203.0.113.20"), addr("192.0.2.10"), addr("192.0.2.11")},
86				table: map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{
87					addr("198.51.100.10"): {dev: "wan0", familyCount: 1},
88					addr("203.0.113.20"):  {dev: "wan1", familyCount: 1},
89					addr("192.0.2.10"):    {dev: "lan0", familyCount: 2},
90					addr("192.0.2.11"):    {dev: "lan0", familyCount: 2},
91				},
92				wantDev: []string{"wan0", "wan1", "", ""},
93			},
94		}
95	
96		for _, tc := range tests {
97			t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
98				got := selectDeviceBinds(tc.srcs, autoModes(len(tc.srcs)), build(tc.table))
99				if len(got) != len(tc.wantDev) {
100					t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds returned %d entries, want %d (%v)", len(got), len(tc.wantDev), got)
101				}
102				for i := range got {
103					if got[i] != tc.wantDev[i] {
104						t.Fatalf("path %d bind = %q, want %q (full: %v want %v)", i, got[i], tc.wantDev[i], got, tc.wantDev)
105					}
106				}
107			})
108		}
109	}
110	
111	// TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedSource is the D38 trap regression (I5): a SOLE path on
112	// a single-address interface is EXACTLY the case BindModeAuto device-binds (see
113	// "solo single-address interface device-binds" above) — and exactly the source-
114	// policy-routed VLAN-per-WAN topology (defects:D38) where a wildcard+device socket
115	// silently defeats `ip rule from <source_addr>`. BindModeSource must force
116	// source-IP pinning here regardless, discriminating it from BindModeAuto on the
117	// identical fixture.
118	func TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedSource(t *testing.T) {
119		addr := netip.MustParseAddr("198.51.100.10")
120		table := map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{addr: {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 1}}
121		resolve := func(a netip.Addr) ifaceInfo { return table[a] }
122	
123		got := selectDeviceBinds([]netip.Addr{addr}, []config.BindMode{config.BindModeSource}, resolve)
124		if len(got) != 1 || got[0] != "" {
125			t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds(BindModeSource) = %v, want [\"\"] (never SO_BINDTODEVICE)", got)
126		}
127	
128		// Sanity: the SAME fixture under BindModeAuto device-binds — confirms the
129		// forced-source result above is actually discriminating the mode, not merely
130		// reflecting an unresolvable/multi-address fixture.
131		gotAuto := selectDeviceBinds([]netip.Addr{addr}, autoModes(1), resolve)
132		if len(gotAuto) != 1 || gotAuto[0] != "eth0" {
133			t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds(BindModeAuto) = %v, want [\"eth0\"] (the D38 trap this test discriminates against)", gotAuto)
134		}
135	}
136	
137	// TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedDevice covers BindModeDevice (I5): it device-binds
138	// whenever the source resolves to an interface — even one BindModeAuto would have
139	// refused (multi-address, or shared with another path) — and falls back to
140	// source-IP binding only when the interface cannot be resolved at all.
141	func TestSelectDeviceBindsForcedDevice(t *testing.T) {
142		addr := func(s string) netip.Addr { return netip.MustParseAddr(s) }
143	
144		t.Run("resolvable multi-address interface still device-binds", func(t *testing.T) {
145			// BindModeAuto refuses this fixture (see "solo multi-address interface ->
146			// source-IP-bound" above); BindModeDevice must device-bind it anyway.
147			src := addr("203.0.113.5")
148			table := map[netip.Addr]ifaceInfo{src: {dev: "eth0", familyCount: 2}}
149			resolve := func(a netip.Addr) ifaceInfo { return table[a] }
150	
151			got := selectDeviceBinds([]netip.Addr{src}, []config.BindMode{config.BindModeDevice}, resolve)
152			if len(got) != 1 || got[0] != "eth0" {
153				t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds(BindModeDevice, multi-address) = %v, want [\"eth0\"]", got)
154			}
155		})
156	
157		t.Run("unresolvable interface falls back to source-IP binding", func(t *testing.T) {
158			src := addr("127.0.0.1") // loopback: interfaceInfo never resolves it (see TestFamilyBindCount's siblings)
159			resolve := func(netip.Addr) ifaceInfo { return ifaceInfo{} }
160	
161			got := selectDeviceBinds([]netip.Addr{src}, []config.BindMode{config.BindModeDevice}, resolve)
162			if len(got) != 1 || got[0] != "" {
163				t.Fatalf("selectDeviceBinds(BindModeDevice, unresolvable) = %v, want [\"\"] (fallback to source-IP binding)", got)
164			}
165		})
166	}
167	
168	// TestPlanPathBinds exercises the real planPathBinds wrapper (real net.Interfaces()
169	// snapshot, no injected resolver) end to end: a loopback source never resolves to a
170	// non-loopback interface, so it source-IP-binds under every mode — the mode plumbing
171	// through planPathBinds compiles and behaves the same as selectDeviceBinds direct.
172	func TestPlanPathBinds(t *testing.T) {
173		srcs := []netip.Addr{netip.MustParseAddr("127.0.0.1")}
174		for _, mode := range []config.BindMode{config.BindModeSource, config.BindModeDevice, config.BindModeAuto} {
175			t.Run(string(mode), func(t *testing.T) {
176				got := planPathBinds(srcs, []config.BindMode{mode})
177				if len(got) != 1 || got[0] != "" {
178					t.Fatalf("planPathBinds(%s, loopback) = %v, want [\"\"]", mode, got)
179				}
180			})
181		}
182	}
183	
184	// TestFamilyBindCount is the D13 regression for interfaceInfo's family-count rule.
185	// An up interface virtually always carries a kernel fe80::/10 link-local alongside
186	// its configured global v6 address; counting the link-local pushed familyCount>=2
187	// so a GLOBAL v6 source never qualified for device binding (T16 re-roam survival).
188	// The kernel never source-selects a link-local for a global destination, so the
189	// link-local must be EXCLUDED from the count of a global v6 source — while a v4
190	// source, and a link-local v6 source, count every same-family address unchanged.
191	func TestFamilyBindCount(t *testing.T) {
192		addr := func(s string) netip.Addr { return netip.MustParseAddr(s) }
193		globalV6 := addr("2001:db8::10")
194		linkLocalV6 := addr("fe80::1")
195		otherGlobalV6 := addr("2001:db8::11")
196	
197		tests := []struct {
198			name       string
199			want       netip.Addr
200			addrs      []netip.Addr
201			wantFamily int
202			wantOwns   bool
203		}{
204			{
205				// The defect case: a global v6 source alongside the kernel link-local.
206				// The link-local must not count, so familyCount == 1 and the source
207				// still qualifies for device binding.
208				name:       "global v6 with kernel link-local excludes link-local",
209				want:       globalV6,
210				addrs:      []netip.Addr{globalV6, linkLocalV6},
211				wantFamily: 1,
212				wantOwns:   true,
213			},
214			{
215				// Two GLOBAL v6 addresses genuinely contend: both count, so the source
216				// does not qualify (a wildcard+device socket could source from either).
217				name:       "two global v6 addresses both count",
218				want:       globalV6,
219				addrs:      []netip.Addr{globalV6, otherGlobalV6, linkLocalV6},
220				wantFamily: 2,
221				wantOwns:   true,
222			},
223			{
224				// A link-local v6 SOURCE is unaffected: every same-family (incl.
225				// link-local) address counts, exactly as before.
226				name:       "link-local v6 source counts link-locals",
227				want:       linkLocalV6,
228				addrs:      []netip.Addr{linkLocalV6, addr("fe80::2")},
229				wantFamily: 2,
230				wantOwns:   true,
231			},
232			{
233				// A v4 source is unaffected by the v6 rule; a coexisting v6 (of either
234				// scope) is a different family and never counts.
235				name:       "v4 source unaffected by v6 addresses",
236				want:       addr("192.0.2.10"),
237				addrs:      []netip.Addr{addr("192.0.2.10"), globalV6, linkLocalV6},
238				wantFamily: 1,
239				wantOwns:   true,
240			},
241		}
242	
243		for _, tc := range tests {
244			t.Run(tc.name, func(t *testing.T) {
245				familyCount, owns := familyBindCount(tc.want, tc.addrs)
246				if familyCount != tc.wantFamily {
247					t.Errorf("familyCount = %d, want %d", familyCount, tc.wantFamily)
248				}
249				if owns != tc.wantOwns {
250					t.Errorf("owns = %v, want %v", owns, tc.wantOwns)
251				}
252			})
253		}
254	}

300	// Multipath is the P1 bonding conn.Bind: one UDP socket per configured path
301	// (bound to the path's source address), all fronted by a SINGLE stable virtual
302	// endpoint the engine holds per peer. Send wraps each opaque WireGuard datagram
303	// in an outer DATA frame (own outer-seq + path-id) and picks a path; one
304	// Bind-owned reader per path unwraps DATA frames into the shared resequencer, and
305	// a SINGLE engine-facing ReceiveFunc drains it and hands the inner WG datagram up
306	// under the shared virtual endpoint (the fan-in that lets paths be added/removed at
307	// runtime without the engine spawning receive goroutines — T30). It replaces
308	// bind.Passthrough behind the same conn.Bind seam (device wiring is unchanged).
309	//
310	// Lifecycle: the sockets live for the duration of an Open→Close span, NOT the
311	// Multipath's whole life. The amneziawg engine calls Close() before every Open()
312	// (device.upLocked → BindUpdate → closeBindLocked, and on IpcSet listen_port /
313	// route-change events) and cycles Close↔Open on Down/Up, so — exactly like
314	// conn.StdNetBind — Open creates the per-path sockets and Close tears them down
315	// AND clears the path state so the next Open rebuilds from scratch. The "closed"
316	// state is simply "no bound sockets" (len(paths)==0); there is no separate sticky
317	// flag that a later Open would have to reset.
318	//
319	// Path policy: an injected sched.Scheduler (T15) chooses the egress path per
320	// datagram. The P1 MVP wires an active-backup scheduler (all traffic on the
321	// preferred primary, transparent failover to a backup on a T13 path-DOWN signal
322	// with failback hysteresis); the weighted/FEC-aware policy (T21) is a different
323	// Scheduler swapped in here with no Bind change. No FEC/resequencing yet
324	// (P2/P3), but every DATA frame carries its outer-seq and path-id so T18/T24 can
325	// consume them.
326	// ProberFactory mints a *telemetry.Prober for a path admitted at runtime (T30),
327	// stamped with the given stable path-id. The device wires it capturing the SAME
328	// per-boot session id, prober config, clock, PSK, and logger the boot-time probers
329	// were built with, so a runtime path's liveness is measured identically to a
330	// boot-time path (its probes join the same session so the peer's reflector adopts
331	// them without a challenge reset). It is nil on a bind built without the probe
332	// transport (the T12 unit tests), which therefore cannot add paths at runtime.
333	type ProberFactory func(name string, id uint8) *telemetry.Prober
334	
335	type Multipath struct {
336		defs []config.Path
337	
338		// classify maps each outbound datagram to its pacer traffic class from the inner
339		// WireGuard message type, parameterized by the tunnel's Amnezia obfuscation profile
340		// so a WireGuard control frame (handshake/keepalive) is recognised — and pacing-
341		// exempted — under advanced security too, not only in vanilla mode (defect D22). It
342		// is immutable after construction and holds no lock, so Send reads it off m.mu.
343		classify wgClassifier
344	
345		// deferredListen binds a reconciled deferred path's socket (T55 background
346		// reconcile). It is an injection seam: the default pins the source IP unless dev is
347		// set (net.ListenUDP / listenPath, matching AddPath's runtime bind — I5), and a test
348		// overrides it to drive the deferred→bound transition deterministically — a
349		// source_addr "becoming assignable" — without a real interface address having to
350		// appear on the host. dev is resolveForcedDeviceBind's decision for the deferred
351		// path's resolved BindMode, computed by reconcileDeferred before the call. Immutable
352		// after construction, never nil.
353		deferredListen func(src netip.Addr, port uint16, dev string) (*net.UDPConn, error)
354	
355		mu sync.Mutex
356	
357		// The PRIMARY peer, embedded so the single-peer datapath (Send, the receive drainer,
358		// the probe loop) and the existing single-peer tests reach its fields — virt,
359		// scheduler, reflector, newProber, probers, sendCodec, paths, outerSeq, resequencer,
360		// fecSend/fecRecv — transparently through promotion, keeping behaviour byte-identical
361		// to the pre-split singleton. It is peers[0]; the concentrator's additional peers live
362		// in peers/peersByName. The former process-global resequencer/outerSeq/scheduler now
363		// live on this peerState, NOT on Multipath.
364		*peerState
365	
366		// peers is every bound peer (len 1 on the single-peer edge/hub). The runtime shared-
367		// path add/remove fan-out iterates it so per-(peer,path) state is created/torn-down for
368		// EVERY currently-bound peer (attachSharedPathLocked / RemovePath). peersByName keys
369		// them by peer id/name.
370		peers       []*peerState
371		peersByName map[string]*peerState
372		// peersView is the LOCK-FREE snapshot of the bound peer set the single engine-facing
373		// receive drainer iterates (newReceiveFunc). m.peers is mutated only under m.mu (peer
374		// wiring / fan-out); every mutation republishes this pointer (republishPeersLocked) so
375		// the drainer enumerates EVERY bound peer's resequencer WITHOUT taking m.mu on the
376		// receive hot path — the same lock-free-publish discipline resequencer/fecRecv use. It
377		// is never nil after construction (the constructor publishes the primary-only view).
378		peersView atomic.Pointer[[]*peerState]
379		// peerByEndpoint is the inbound endpoint-keyed demux placeholder (still unused today: the
380		// single-peer edge/hub needs no demux, and the source-keyed binding below is what the
381		// concentrator receive path routes on).
382		peerByEndpoint map[netip.AddrPort]*peerState
383		// peerBySource is the inbound source-demux map: a learned source address → the peer it was
384		// bound to by an authenticated PROBE (T88). The concentrator's per-socket readLoops resolve
385		// a datagram's source to its owning peer through it; a source is bound ONLY on the first
386		// PROBE that MAC-verifies under a peer's psk (D9/D11: bindings, like remotes, are learned
387		// only from authenticated PROBEs). It is published copy-on-write through an atomic.Pointer
388		// so the receive hot path resolves a bound source with a lock-free Load (no m.mu — a reader
389		// must never block on m.mu, since Close waits on the readers WHILE holding it), and a new
390		// binding is installed lock-free by a CAS republish of a copy with the entry added
391		// (bindSourceToPeer). Nil until the first binding; the single-peer edge/hub never consults
392		// it (one peer owns every socket — handleInbound's fast path skips the demux entirely).
393		peerBySource atomic.Pointer[map[netip.Addr]*peerState]
394		// maxDemuxSources caps peerBySource (the provisional/unbound-source demux state) so a
395		// bootstrap flood cannot grow it without bound (Q26/Q27, see defaultMaxDemuxSources). Set
396		// once at construction and read on the lock-free bind path (bindSourceToPeer); a test may
397		// lower it to exercise cap-exhaustion. Zero (never set) means "no cap".
398		maxDemuxSources int

624	func (m *Multipath) Open(port uint16) ([]ReceiveFunc, uint16, error) {
625		m.mu.Lock()
626		defer m.mu.Unlock()
627		if len(m.paths) != 0 {
628			return nil, 0, conn.ErrBindAlreadyOpen
629		}
630	
631		// (Re)build the per-Open datapath planes — send Codec, receive resequencer, and FEC
632		// send/receive state — fresh for this bring-up, for EVERY bound peer (not just the
633		// primary via promotion). This keeps Open symmetric with closeSocketsLocked, which
634		// clears every peer's per-Open state: a concentrator peer bound before Open must get
635		// its OWN fresh planes on each Close→Open cycle, and one peer's (re)creation must never
636		// touch another peer's release point or FEC group state. On the single-peer edge/hub
637		// m.peers holds only the primary, so this is byte-identical to the pre-split rebuild.
638		// See openPeerDatapathLocked.
639		for _, p := range m.peers {
640			if err := m.openPeerDatapathLocked(p); err != nil {
641				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
642				return nil, 0, err
643			}
644		}
645	
646		// Resolve every path's interface up front and decide, per path, whether its
647		// socket may be device-bound (SO_BINDTODEVICE + wildcard — survives a T16
648		// re-roam) or must pin the specific source IP (the pre-T16 behaviour, required
649		// when paths share an interface or the interface is multi-address, so distinct
650		// specific-IP sockets coexist on one port without an EADDRINUSE collision). See
651		// selectDeviceBinds.
652		srcs := make([]netip.Addr, len(m.defs))
653		modes := make([]config.BindMode, len(m.defs))
654		for i := range m.defs {
655			srcs[i] = m.defs[i].SourceAddr
656			modes[i] = m.defs[i].Bind
657		}
658		bindDevs := planPathBinds(srcs, modes)
659	
660		// A path whose WELL-FORMED source_addr is merely NOT-YET-ASSIGNABLE at boot
661		// (EADDRNOTAVAIL: no interface holds the address — a 5G modem without a DHCP
662		// lease, Starlink mid-obstruction) is DEFERRED rather than treated as fatal, so
663		// the bond comes up on the paths that DO bind (G2/W1, approach A). Tolerating it
664		// requires the runtime path-down machinery: the deferred path's prober stays
665		// StateDown so the scheduler excludes it, and a DynamicScheduler whose membership
666		// can be reconciled to only the bound paths. Absent the probe transport (the T12
667		// no-prober unit binds) or a dynamic scheduler there is no such Down model, so —
668		// exactly as AddPath refuses a runtime add without probers — every bind error
669		// stays fatal there. A MALFORMED source_addr never reaches here (config.validate
670		// rejects it at load), and any OTHER bind error (EADDRINUSE, permission) is fatal.
671		dyn, dynOK := m.scheduler.(sched.DynamicScheduler)
672		tolerateDefer := m.probers != nil && dynOK
673		m.deferred = nil
674	
675		actualPort := port
676		firstBound := true
677		// boundProbers is index-aligned with m.paths (the paths that actually bound), so
678		// the scheduler reconcile below and Send's Pick->m.paths[idx] mapping stay aligned
679		// even when a middle path deferred. nil in the no-prober case (nothing to reconcile).
680		var boundProbers []*telemetry.Prober
681		for i := range m.defs {
682			def := m.defs[i]
683			// Device-bind this path when selectDeviceBinds proved it safe (so a
684			// mid-session source-address change / T16 re-roam does not break the socket),
685			// otherwise pin the specific source IP. See selectDeviceBinds / listenPath.
686			c, err := listenPath(def.SourceAddr, port, bindDevs[i])
687			if err != nil {
688				if tolerateDefer && errors.Is(err, syscall.EADDRNOTAVAIL) {
689					// Defer this path: record its def + boot prober (kept Down) for the T55
690					// background reconcile to retry, and leave the bond to come up on the rest.
691					// Mirrors AddPath's rollback discipline — a failed path never disturbs the
692					// tunnel — at the boot boundary.
693					m.deferred = append(m.deferred, deferredPath{def: def, prober: m.probers[i]})
694					continue
695				}
696				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
697				return nil, 0, fmt.Errorf("bind: open path %q on %s: %w", def.Name, def.SourceAddr, err)
698			}
699			// Large SO_RCVBUF, best-effort: the kernel caps at net.core.rmem_max and
700			// SetReadBuffer does not require privilege, so a returned error is rare;
701			// treat it as non-fatal rather than refusing to bind in a restricted env.
702			_ = c.SetReadBuffer(socketRecvBuffer)
703	
704			// The path binds to the primary peer here; its receive codec is that peer's codec
705			// (reached through the embedded primary, as the datapath reaches m.sendCodec).
706			codec, err := m.newCodec()
707			if err != nil {
708				_ = c.Close()
709				_ = m.closeSocketsLocked()
710				return nil, 0, err
711			}
712	
713			shared := &sharedPathState{name: def.Name, id: uint8(i), src: def.SourceAddr, conn: c}
714			ps := &peerPathState{sharedPathState: shared, peer: m.peerState, codec: codec}
715			if m.probers != nil {
716				ps.prober = m.probers[i]
717				// Reconcile the DATA-frame path-id to the prober's IMMUTABLE stamp rather than
718				// to the slice index i: after a runtime RemovePath the survivor keeps its
719				// original (higher) stamp, so index-based numbering would renumber a live path
720				// AND diverge its DATA id from its PROBE stamp. Taking id from the prober keeps
721				// DATA and PROBE agreeing on the wire and a survivor's id stable across a reopen.
722				shared.id = ps.prober.PathID()
723				boundProbers = append(boundProbers, ps.prober)
724			}
725			switch {
726			case def.DestAddr.IsValid():
727				ps.setRemote(def.DestAddr)
728			case m.hasDefaultRemote:
729				ps.setRemote(m.defaultRemote)
730			}
731			m.shared = append(m.shared, shared)
732			m.paths = append(m.paths, ps)
733			// Publish the primary's view for the receive demux (T88). On the single-peer edge/hub
734			// this is the ONLY view, which handleInbound reads as the byte-identical fast path; a
735			// concentrator peer bound later appends its own view to the same socket.
736			shared.addViewLocked(ps)
737			if firstBound {
738				actualPort = uint16(c.LocalAddr().(*net.UDPAddr).Port)
739				firstBound = false
740			}
741		}
742	
743		// Hard guard (a): a bond with NO transport is impossible. If every configured path

# Agent instructions — wanbond

Instructions for AI agents (and humans) working in this repo. Read the
[README](README.md) for what wanbond is and [docs/design.md](docs/design.md) for
the architecture before changing anything.

## Keep the docs current (required)

**Documentation is part of the definition of done.** When a change alters
behavior, configuration, architecture, the wire format, an invariant, or an
operational step, update the affected docs **in the same change**:

- **[README.md](README.md)** — the front door: capabilities, quick start, repo
  layout, status/limitations. Update when a capability, config surface, package,
  or limitation changes.
- **[docs/design.md](docs/design.md)** — architecture and what we built on top of
  amneziawg-go. Update when the data path, a layer/package, an invariant, the
  security model, or a deliberate boundary changes.
- **[docs/install.md](docs/install.md)** — setup and operation. Update when
  config keys, systemd units, firewall/provisioning steps, or metrics change.
- **[docs/manual-checklist.md](docs/manual-checklist.md)** — update when a
  verification step or a real-link measurement changes.

A change that moves the code but not the docs it invalidates is **incomplete**.
If you are unsure whether a doc is affected, grep it for the symbol/keyword you
touched. Prefer editing the existing doc over adding a new one; keep docs
consistent with each other (e.g. a config key described in README, design, and
install must match).

## Build, test, lint

The dev shell (`nix develop`) puts Go 1.26, golangci-lint, and the netem/DPI
tooling on `PATH`. Run tools from it (e.g. `nix develop --command bash -c '…'`).

```sh
just build      # go build ./...
just test       # unprivileged unit/property tests (go test ./...)
just lint       # go vet + golangci-lint, INCLUDING -tags e2e and -tags realhosts
just e2e        # privileged netns fixture: sudo -E go test -tags e2e ./test/e2e/...
just realhosts  # opt-in real-machine tier (-tags realhosts), report-only
```

The non-privileged gate a change must pass before merge:

```sh
go build ./... && go vet ./... && test -z "$(gofmt -l cmd internal test)" && go test ./...
```

`-tags e2e` / `-tags realhosts` need root / real hosts and are **not** part of the
default gate; validate them separately (see Testing discipline).

## Load-bearing invariants — do not break

Full detail in [docs/design.md §Load-bearing invariants](docs/design.md). In
short:

1. The engine sees **one virtual endpoint per peer**; the Bind fans out beneath
   it (design rule A1). No per-packet endpoint churn to the engine.
2. Use wanbond's **own outer sequence space**; never reuse/perturb the inner
   WireGuard counter.
3. **Resequence before** the inner anti-replay window validates.
4. Inner fail-closed; **PROBE/CONTROL are PSK-HMAC authenticated** with monotonic
   anti-replay; DATA/PARITY are unauthenticated **by design** (DoS-grade risk
   accepted — do not "fix" this without a design decision).
5. All engine (`conn`) coupling stays isolated to **`internal/bind/bind.go`** —
   preserve the fork-swap hedge.
6. Amnezia config is **all-or-nothing** and the engine is **single-per-process**
   (package-level globals). Keep the config validation that enforces this.
7. On **any `klauspost/reedsolomon` (or amneziawg-go) version bump**, re-verify
   `TestKlauspostParityPrefixStableInvariant` (`internal/fec`) before landing —

Closed the coverage gap: split `resolveForcedDeviceBind` (`internal/bind/pathsock.go`) into a thin real-`net.Interfaces()` wrapper plus a pure, unit-testable `selectForcedDeviceBind` decision function (mirroring `planPathBinds`/`selectDeviceBinds`); added a `Multipath.resolveDeviceBind` injection seam (mirroring `deferredListen`) so `AddPath` (`internal/bind/multipath.go`) and `reconcileDeferred` (`internal/bind/reconcile.go`) route through it instead of calling the free function directly.

Added `TestResolveForcedDeviceBindAgreesWithRealInterfaces`, which discovers a real non-loopback single-address interface on the host (this sandbox has `eth-mtk`) and skips gracefully if none exists. I manually performed the mutation, confirmed this new test fails with the exact expected message, then reverted and reran the full gate green — the mutation-verify step the criticism specified.
- **Issues:** none. `TestResolveForcedDeviceBindAgreesWithRealInterfaces` is best-effort/skippable on a loopback-only host (e.g. a maximally isolated CI container) since Go cannot fake `net.Interface.Addrs()` without a real interface — this mirrors the existing `TestPlanPathBinds` precedent in the same file, which also only sanity-checks the real-interfaces wrapper trivially.
